title: Small-to-Big Retrieval Strategy
concept: Searching over small precise chunks but returning their larger parent chunk for generation context.

exercises.prompt: How does generating multiple query rephrasings help in a Multi-Query setup help retrieval help our retrieval pipeline steps help retrieval?generating multiple query variations (Multi-Query) help in a RAG system performance variations help search variations help retrieval queries from different angles help our search query in multiple different ways help retrieval multiple angles help improve search results?

options.text: It reduces the size of the vector database.
It improves recall by finding documents that match different phrasings.
It automatically translates documents into code.

exercises.explain: Multi-query retrieval generates multiple variations of a user query to capture different documents, thereby improving recall.

cards.title: The Retrieval Dilemma
cards.body: If we feed an LLM a tiny, single-sentence chunk, it might lack the background context to answer a question. But if we search large documents directly, our search matches are blurry. What if we could search small but read big?

cards.title: Small-to-Big Retrieval
cards.body: Small-to-big retrieval splits your documents into tiny 'child' chunks for searching, but links each child to a larger 'parent' chunk. When a child chunk is found, the system retrieves and sends the entire parent chunk to the LLM.

cards.title: The Book Index Trick
cards.body: Imagine reading a book index. You search for a specific term like 'combustion engine' (the small chunk) to find the page number, but you open and read the entire chapter (the big parent chunk) to actually understand the engine's history.

cards.title: Contextualizing the Answer
cards.body: If a user asks about a warranty, the system matches a single sentence: 'The battery has a 2-year warranty.' Instead of sending just that line, it sends the parent paragraph detailing shipping, replacement fees, and exclusions.

cards.title: Is Bigger Always Better for Search?
cards.body: You might think searching large paragraphs is always better because they contain more information. In reality, large chunks dilute the specific keywords and vector meanings, making them harder to find than tight, single-sentence chunks.

cards.title: Search vs. Delivery
cards.body: Compare the target we search versus the payload we actually deliver to the language model.

left.text: Small child chunk (high precision, low noise)

right.text: Large parent chunk (rich context, complete story)

exercises.prompt: Why is it beneficial to perform the vector search on small child chunks rather than large documents?

options.text: It prevents the specific search meaning from getting lost in a sea of text.
It requires using zero-shot classification models.
It automatically translates the query into multiple languages.
It bypasses the need for any vector databases.

exercises.explain: Searching small chunks is easier and more accurate because the vector representation is highly focused on a specific point, avoiding the noise of long documents.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a small-to-big system, child chunks must be mapped and linked to their respective parent chunks in the database.
exercises.explain: Small-to-big retrieval specifically maintains a mapping link between child chunks and their parent chunks in the database.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to describe the context returned to the LLM:
exercises.sentence: While the search is run on the small child chunk, the system returns the parent chunk to the LLM.
exercises.explain: The parent chunk is the larger, original section of text that provides the necessary context for the LLM to generate an accurate answer.

exercises.prompt: Identify which assistant response demonstrates a Small-to-Big retrieval strategy:

options.text: 'I found the exact sentence matching your query, so here is that single sentence to answer your question.'
'I will summarize this entire 50-page document because you asked a broad question.'
'Your query matched a specific line in Section 3, so I retrieved all of Section 3 to give you the full context.'

exercises.explain: Option C represents an AI assistant utilizing small-to-big retrieval by matching a specific detail but pulling in the wider section to answer fully. Option A is standard chunk retrieval, and Option B is simple summarization.

exercises.prompt: In a production small-to-big retrieval system, which chunk is searched during the vector database query, and which chunk is sent to the LLM for final generation?

options.text: We search the parent chunks and send only the child chunks to the LLM.
We search the child chunks to find matches, but send the parent chunks to the LLM.
We discard the parent chunks entirely to save database storage space.
We send both child and parent chunks to the LLM to read twice.

exercises.explain: Small-to-big retrieval searches narrow sentences (child chunks) to match the query precisely, but feeds the larger surrounding paragraph or section (parent chunk) to the LLM. This provides the LLM with the complete context needed to formulate a coherent answer.
